persons.arguments_against: The strongest counter-hypothesis is a same-name son or relative: the bio's appointee of 1872 could have retired in the 1880s and been succeeded in the same surveyorship by a son sharing the initials A. W. H., which would make the 1890-1898 Antigua stint and the 1897-1899 Leeward Islands stint a different man. This fails for several reasons. The biography pins this exact, specialized post to this man ('Surveyor of public works, Antigua, 8 April, 1872'), and the staff lists show one continuous incumbent of the identical post ('Surveyor of Public Works, Roads, &c.') under the identical three initials from 1883 through 1899 with no break, no overlap, and no second A. W. H. A'Court ever listed simultaneously — a father/son pair sharing all three initials and the same specialized office seamlessly is far less parsimonious than one career. The 'Hon.' prefix on the bio is matched in the staff lists (1883 and 1898) and is explained by the 1897 'Legislative Council (Local)' membership, tying the stints together as one person. The mechanical colony FAILs on the two Leeward Islands stints are an artifact: Antigua was a presidency of the Leeward Islands federation, so the same Antigua establishment was printed under the federation heading in some editions; the position string is verbatim identical across all three stints. A second trap — honoured retiree kept on the list — does not apply because the listings carry an active Civil Establishment post, not a courtesy entry. Corpus context (only 4 officials with this rare surname) further limits the homonym pool, and the only differently-initialed form ('H') has been left unassigned.

evidence_cited.fact: Surveyor of public works, Antigua, 8 April, 1872.

persons.would_overturn: A printed birth/death year or obituary showing the 1872 appointee died or left the service before 1890; a second biography or staff-list entry showing two distinct A. W. H. A'Courts listed simultaneously in different posts; or evidence that the Antigua surveyorship passed to a son of the same initials (e.g., a Record of Services entry dating a NEW appointment to the post in the 1880s-90s).

unassigned_stints.reason: Single printed initial 'H' against the bio's 'A. W. H.'. The exact post ('Surveyor of Public Works, Roads, &c.'), the 'Hon.' style, and the bio's 8 April 1872 appointment date make identity plausible (the same incumbent with initials partially printed), but a same-surname relative — e.g., a father named H. A'Court holding the post in 1877-79 — is a realistic counter-hypothesis, and the corpus contains a second A'Court biography not included in this case that could hold this stint. Per the single-initial caution and the no-false-merges directive, left unlinked for human review.

notes: The mechanical colony/tenure/edition FAILs on the Leeward Islands stints are artifacts of Antigua being a presidency of the Leeward Islands federation: the identical position string 'Surveyor of Public Works, Roads, &c.' appears under both colony headings across editions, and the 1890 Antigua stint co-occurs with the bio's 1890 edition. The unassigned 1877-79 'Hon. H. A'Court' stint is very plausibly the same man (the bio dates his appointment to this exact post to 1872, before the stint begins) and is the natural start of the same continuous 1877-1899 career; recommend a human reviewer check the second A'Court biography in the corpus before linking it.
